mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Canada on?
mapQuestions.answer: North America. Canada is the northern half of the continent, sharing one of the longest borders in the world with the USA 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Canada?
mapQuestions.answer: Ottawa, in the province of Ontario. Most people guess Toronto, but Ottawa is the capital. It sits on the border between the English-speaking and French-speaking parts of the country.

mapQuestions.question: Three oceans touch Canada. Which ones?
mapQuestions.answer: The Pacific (west), the Atlantic (east), and the Arctic (north) 🌊 Canada has the longest coastline of any country in the world.

mapQuestions.question: How big is Canada compared to other countries?
mapQuestions.answer: Canada is the second-largest country in the world by area, after Russia. But only about 40 million people live in it, so a lot of it is wild.

mapQuestions.question: How many official languages does Canada have, and what are they?
mapQuestions.answer: Two. English and French. Around 22% of Canadians grow up speaking French as a first language, mostly in the province of Quebec.

foods.name: Poutine
foods.description: Hot chips topped with fresh squeaky cheese curds and brown gravy. Invented in Quebec in the 1950s. Sounds wrong, tastes glorious.

foods.name: Pancakes with maple syrup
foods.description: Stacks of fluffy pancakes drowned in real Canadian maple syrup, ideally with crispy bacon on the side.

foods.name: Butter tart
foods.description: A small pastry shell filled with butter, sugar, syrup and eggs, baked until the filling is gooey and sticky. Pure sugar happiness.

foods.name: Tim Hortons coffee + Timbit
foods.description: The chain that almost every Canadian grew up on. Coffee with cream, and "Timbits" (the small round bits cut from the middle of donuts).

foods.name: Montreal smoked meat
foods.description: Beef brisket cured with spices for over a week, smoked, then steamed and piled onto rye bread with yellow mustard. Found in delis across Montreal.

foods.name: Atlantic lobster roll
foods.description: Sweet chunks of fresh-caught lobster meat in a buttery split-top bun. The best are eaten in tiny harbour villages in the east, dripping butter down your hands.

scavengerItems.label: A maple leaf on a flag, shop sign or coin
A Canada goose (huge bird with a black head and white cheeks)
A beaver lodge in a lake, or a "beware of beavers" sign
Kids playing ice hockey, or a hockey stick in a porch
A "beware of moose / elk" road sign
A toboggan or sled propped against a house in winter
A really colourful maple tree in autumn (red, orange, yellow)
A bilingual sign that says "STOP / ARRÊT" (Quebec)
An Inuit soapstone carving or an inukshuk (stacked rock figure)
A tall grain elevator beside a railway line on the prairies

geoMatches.description: The biggest city, on Lake Ontario. The CN Tower used to be the tallest building in the world. More than half the people in Toronto were born outside Canada.
A walled French-speaking city on the St Lawrence River. Cobbled streets, French cafés, the only fortified city north of Mexico. UNESCO-listed.
A mountain town in the Canadian Rockies in Alberta. Turquoise glacier lakes (Lake Louise, Moraine), bears, mountain goats, and hot springs.
Three huge waterfalls on the border between Canada and the USA. Around 168,000 cubic metres of water plunge over them every minute. You can ride a boat into the spray.
In the far north-west, near Alaska. Wild forests, gold-rush ghost towns, the northern lights in winter, and mountains taller than the Alps.
A big rocky island off the east coast. Atlantic puffins, icebergs floating past in spring, and the friendliest accents in the country.

senses.smell: Maple smoke from a wood stove, pine forests after rain, snow in the cold air, freshly baked donuts at Tim Hortons, lake water at dawn, woodfire on a campsite…
senses.hear: A Canada goose honking overhead, "eh?" tagged on a sentence, hockey sticks slapping on ice, French and English mixed at the same shop counter, loons calling on a lake at night…
senses.taste: Sweet maple syrup on bacon, salty poutine gravy, double-double coffee, gooey butter tarts, fresh Atlantic lobster, ice-cold lake water…
senses.feel: Cold snow biting your cheeks, a warm tuque pulled down to your eyebrows, sticky maple syrup on your fingers, wet socks from skating, hot mittens vs the freezing air outside…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Canada so obsessed with maple syrup?
stories.body: Canada makes around 70% of the world's maple syrup. It is made from the sap of sugar maple trees, harvested in early spring when warm days and freezing nights make the sap run through the trees. Small spouts are tapped into the trunks and the sap is collected in buckets. It takes about 40 LITRES of clear sap to boil down into 1 litre of golden syrup. Canada even has a "Strategic Maple Syrup Reserve" with over 50 million litres stored as backup, in case of a bad harvest. In 2011 thieves broke into the reserve and stole around 18 million dollars worth of syrup over months, by emptying barrels and refilling them with water. It became one of the biggest heists in Canadian history.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your country had a "strategic reserve" of one food, what would yours be, and why?

stories.question: Why is there a town where polar bears walk past the houses?
stories.body: Churchill is a small town on the shore of Hudson Bay in northern Canada, famous as the "polar bear capital of the world". In October and November, hundreds of polar bears wander into town as they wait for the bay to freeze over so they can hunt seals out on the ice. Locals keep their car doors unlocked, so people can jump inside if a bear appears. Schools have polar-bear-safety drills the way other schools have fire drills. There is also a "polar bear jail", a holding facility for bears that wander too close, which keeps them safely fed until the ice freezes and they can be released.

stories.question: Why do some Canadians speak French and some English?
stories.body: In the 1500s and 1600s, French explorers and traders settled the area along the St Lawrence River, founding cities like Quebec City and Montreal. Then in 1763 the British took over and English-speaking settlers arrived in huge numbers. But the French communities never went away. Today around 22% of Canadians grow up speaking French, mostly in the province of Quebec. Federal law says cereal boxes, signs and government documents have to be printed in both English and French. The big French city is Montreal, the second-biggest French-speaking city in the world after Paris. If you cross the river from Ottawa into Gatineau, the language on the signs changes immediately.

stories.question: What were Canada's residential schools?
stories.body: Indigenous peoples — First Nations, Inuit and Métis — have lived in what is now Canada for at least 14,000 years. From the 1830s to the 1990s, the Canadian government forced more than 150,000 Indigenous children to attend "residential schools", boarding schools run mostly by churches, designed to "kill the Indian in the child". Children were taken from their families, forbidden to speak their languages, often abused, and many died and were never returned home. In recent years, the unmarked graves of thousands of children have been found at former school sites. Canada has officially apologised, declared a national day of remembrance, and is still doing the long work of "truth and reconciliation". Many Canadian schools now begin every day with a "land acknowledgement" naming the First Nation whose territory the school sits on.
stories.thinkingQuestion: When a country does something terrible to its own people, how do you think it should try to make it right years later?

stories.question: Why didn't Canada become part of the USA?
stories.body: When the 13 American colonies fought their War of Independence in 1776, the British colonies that are now Canada chose to stay loyal to Britain. Tens of thousands of people who had fought on the British side, the "Loyalists", fled north when America won. Twice (in 1775 and 1812) American armies invaded north, hoping Canadians would welcome them. Both times they were pushed back. Canada became its own country peacefully in 1867 — it never fought a war of independence against Britain. Canada and the USA share the longest unguarded border in the world, almost 9,000 km long, and the two countries are deeply similar. But Canada has universal healthcare, much stricter gun rules, the British monarch on its money, the metric system, and it tends to describe itself as a "mosaic" of cultures rather than a "melting pot".

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten in Canada so far?
What's the most surprising thing you have put maple syrup on?
Have you heard French and English in the same place yet?
Canada gets to −40°C in winter and +30°C in summer. What's the most extreme weather you have had?
Have you seen any wild animals so far? (bears? moose? geese?)
Canada and the USA are so similar — what's one thing that has felt totally different here?

wordSearch.hint: Canadian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Canada goose
animals.description: Honking V-formations overhead in spring and autumn, lakes in summer.

animals.name: Eastern chipmunk
animals.description: Stripey small ground squirrels in every campground and park.

animals.name: Beaver
animals.description: Canada's national animal. Dams across streams, especially in Algonquin and the Rockies.

animals.name: White-tailed deer
animals.description: At dusk in fields, gardens, even Toronto ravines.

animals.name: Bald eagle
animals.description: In British Columbia and the Atlantic provinces, especially around salmon runs.

animals.name: Black bear or grizzly
animals.description: In the Rockies, Algonquin, BC coast. Don't leave food in your tent!

animals.name: Moose
animals.description: The biggest deer in the world. In Newfoundland, Algonquin, the Rockies, the Yukon.
